CIFAR-10 Classification project CNN¶

In [10]:
# fix for memory issues
import tensorflow as tf
gpus = tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU')
if gpus:
    for gpu in gpus:
        tf.config.experimental.set_memory_growth(gpu, True)
In [11]:
import keras
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Input, Dense, Flatten, Dropout, BatchNormalization, Conv2D, MaxPooling2D
from tensorflow.keras import layers
from tensorflow.keras.utils import to_categorical
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Data preparation¶

Load data and scale the values to 0.0-1.0 for better performance, no need to reshape for CNN

In [12]:
# Load data
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = keras.datasets.cifar10.load_data()
# Reshape and scale to 0.0-1.0
x_train = x_train.astype("float32")/255
x_test = x_test.astype("float32") /255
In [13]:
print(x_train.shape, y_train.shape)
print(x_test.shape, y_test.shape)

(50000, 32, 32, 3) (50000, 1)
(10000, 32, 32, 3) (10000, 1)

Model¶

In [14]:
# Data augmentation module
data_augmentation = Sequential([
    layers.RandomFlip("horizontal"),
    layers.RandomRotation(0.1),
    layers.RandomZoom(0.1),
])
In [15]:
# Build layers
model = Sequential([
    Input(shape=(32, 32, 3)),
    data_augmentation,
    Conv2D(32, (3, 3), padding="same", activation='relu'), 
    BatchNormalization(),
    Conv2D(32, (3, 3), padding="same", activation='relu'), 
    BatchNormalization(),
    MaxPooling2D((2, 2)), 

    Conv2D(64, (3, 3), padding="same", activation='relu'),  
    BatchNormalization(),
    Conv2D(64, (3, 3), padding="same", activation='relu'),  
    BatchNormalization(),
    MaxPooling2D((2, 2)), 

    Conv2D(128, (3, 3), padding="same", activation='relu'), 
    BatchNormalization(),
    Conv2D(128, (3, 3), padding="same", activation='relu'),  
    BatchNormalization(),
    MaxPooling2D((2, 2)), 
    
    Flatten(),
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.3),
    Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])
In [16]:
# Model options
model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss=keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy(),
              metrics=['accuracy'],
)
In [17]:
# Training options
history = model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=50, batch_size=32, validation_split=0.2)

 Total params: 1,259,968 (4.81 MB)

 Trainable params: 419,690 (1.60 MB)

 Non-trainable params: 896 (3.50 KB)

 Optimizer params: 839,382 (3.20 MB)

Model tuning methodology¶

Did a lot of different variations of the model. Tried increasing layers/cells to increase accuracy, but overfitting became a problem with when trying to increase epochs. Started with a few CNN-cells with max pooling between. One extra dense layer at the end helped with accuracy. Tried adding dropouts, but multiple dropouts seemed to make validation loss very erratic. Batch normalization helped with accuracy and overfitting enough to be able to add more epochs to get much better accuracy. To add more convolution layers had to add padding to the layers so the pictures don't get too small and not max pool between every layer(basicly 3 max pool layers is the maximum for 32x32 pictures since that already makes them 4x4). Added data augmentation with flip, rotation and zoom which increased accuracy even more (data augmentation also increased training time drasticly).

In short the goal was to add enough overfitting reducing qualities to be able to train longer with more layers to get the best accuracy.

Analysis¶

From the example predictions results below we see that for a lot of the pictures the model is very confident(>90%) on it's prediction. We see this in pictures 7 and 10 where it's very confidently predicting wrong. In the pictures with more mixed predictions there's a pattern of very unusual pictures of target category e.g. picture 18 is just a horse's head and picture 12 is a side profile of a cat which would be hard to differentiate from many other animals.

Comparison between CNN and FCN¶

The CNN model achieved much better accuracy on the test set (84% vs 51%). Learning/loss curves for the CNN-model is much steeper because it "gained" more accuracy. The CNN-models validation loss curve was smoother than the FCN-models. The training accuracy is also much closer to the test accuracy in the CNN-model indicating less overfitting and more reliable results. The FNC-model had 3.8 million trainable parameters while the CNN-model had 400 000 parameters. The discrepancy would be even larger with bigger pictures since in a FCN each pixel has it's own trainable parameters whereas in CNN the filter's have paramateres not individual pictures. Even so the CNN-model was much slower to train ~35 minutes comapred to ~4 minutes of FCN largely because it went through more epochs, but also because it had more layers and the data augmentation slowed training by ~2x-3x.

CNN is better than FNC for picture recognition because a pixel doesn't lose the context of any of the other pixels next to it and the shapes it finds are not tied to a specific location in the picture. It specificly relies on nearby/adjacent pixels to find features in a picture by finding patterns(e.g. horizontal or diagonal lines etc.). Where FNC basicly just tries to find the relationships between pixels in a 1d vector, CNN can find patterns between nearby pixels.

In [26]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = x_test
start = 5000
# only predict needed ones since VRAM ran out :(
y_pred = model.predict(x[start:start+20], batch_size=1)
labels = ["airplane","automobile","bird","cat","deer","dog","frog","horse","ship","truck"]

for k in range(20):
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 2))
    
    # Show image
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
    plt.imshow(x[start+k].reshape((32, 32, 3)))
    plt.axis("off")
    true_label_idx = int(y_test[start+k]) if y_test.ndim == 1 else int(y_test[start+k, 0])
    plt.title(f"{k+1}. {labels[true_label_idx]}")

    # Show bar graph
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
    probs = y_pred[k].flatten()

    # Color the correct label green
    colors = ["green" if i == true_label_idx else "blue" for i in range(10)]
    plt.bar(range(10), probs, color=colors)
    plt.xticks(range(10), labels, rotation=45)
    plt.ylim([0, 1])
    plt.title("Predicted probabilities")
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

20/20 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 8ms/step
In [29]:
# Save model
model.save('./CIFAR_10_CNN.keras')
